data.title: Key deals this week: Organon, UMG, Whitestone REIT, Gilead and more
data.description:
Here's a list of key deals reported across sectors this week:

  • Organon (OGN) climbed ~23% on Friday, on track to record its best-ever intraday rally after media reports from India indicated that Mumbai-based generic drugmaker Sun Pharmaceutical Industries had submitted a $12B binding offer for the U.S. company.
  • Pershing Square Capital Management submitted a non-binding proposal to buy all outstanding shares of Universal Music Group N.V. (UMGNF), offering €9.4B in cash, or €5.05 per share, and 0.77 shares of the new UMG stock per existing share.
  • Gemini Space Station (GEMI) soared 11% on a report that the crypto exchange is seeing interest from potential buyers for parts of its business.
  • G Mining Ventures (GMINF) said Thursday it agreed to acquire G2 Goldfields (GUYGF) in an all-stock deal valued at ~C$3B that effectively combines adjacent gold projects in Guyana to create a large-scale mining hub.
  • Whitestone REIT (WSR) has entered into a definitive merger agreement with Ares Real Estate (ARES) funds to be acquired for $19/share in an all-cash transaction valued at around $1.7B.
  • American Ocean Minerals and Odyssey Marine Exploration (OMEX) on Wednesday said they have agreed to merge in an all-stock transaction valuing the combined company at about $1 billion.
  • Gilead (GILD) said on Tuesday that it would acquire Germany-based biotechnology firm Tubulis GmbH for $5B to build on its oncology pipeline.
  • Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX) has agreed to acquire Soleno Therapeutics (SLNO) for $53 per share in cash, valuing the deal at approximately $2.9B.

data.title: OpenAI says user data is safe after security issue involving third-party tool
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) announced on Friday that there is no evidence to suggest a security issue linked to a third-party developer tool called Axios in March has compromised its user data.

In a statement, the Microsoft (MSFT)-backed AI startup said that the issue followed an attack by a North Korea-linked group on Axios, a widely used third-party developer library, as part of a broader security incident on Mar. 31.

“We found no evidence that OpenAI user data was accessed, that our systems or intellectual property was compromised, or that our software was altered," the company said.

The attack had caused the GitHub Actions workflow used by OpenAI in the macOS app-signing process to download and execute a malicious version of Axios. The workflow had access to a signing certificate, used by the company to inform customers that its macOS applications, including ChatGPT and Codex, originate from OpenAI.

While the incident had no impact on the signing certificate, the company said that, however, “out of an abundance of caution, we are treating the certificate as compromised and are revoking and rotating it.”

As a result, OpenAI stated that older versions of its macOS desktop apps will no longer get updates or support and may not function, effective May 8. “The security and privacy of your information are a top priority. We’re committed to being transparent and taking quick action when issues arise.”

data.title: Neuropsychiatric drug developer Seaport files for U.S. IPO
data.description:
Seaport Therapeutics (SPTX), a clinical-stage biotech focused on developing drugs for neuropsychiatric diseases such as depression, has submitted regulatory filings to the SEC for its initial public offering in the U.S.

Founded by biotech veteran Daphne Zohar in 2024, Seaport (SPTX) seeks to list on the Nasdaq Global Market under the symbol “SPTX,” with plans to use IPO proceeds for multiple operational activities, including R&D work related to its lead asset, GlyphAllo.

GlyphAllo, an oral prodrug of the naturally occurring stress-reducing agent allopregnanolone, is currently undergoing a Phase 2 trial called BUOY-1 for major depressive disorder, with its topline results expected in H1 2027.

Zohar, who serves as the CEO and a board member of the Boston, Massachusetts-based firm, is a founder of PureTech (PRTC), which holds roughly a 43% stake in the company. PureTech (PRTC)-founded Karuna Therapeutics was acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) for $14B in 2024.

With no commercialized products, Seaport (SPTX) has yet to turn a profit since its inception. As of Dec. 31, 2025, the company had an accumulated deficit of $114.1M, and its net loss in 2025 rose ~60% YoY to $74.9M.

data.title: SA Asks: Which AI company has the most viable business plan?
data.description:
Which AI company has the most viable business plan?

Seeking Alpha analysts Bohdan Kucheriavyi and Rick Orford offer their thoughts.

Bohdan Kucheriavyi: Nvidia (NVDA) stands out as a clear winner for me. By capturing the majority of the AI accelerator market and releasing more advanced chips regularly, Nvidia is able to stay ahead of its competitors and benefit the most from record spending on AI by companies and governments across the globe.

More importantly, Nvidia can absorb a significant portion of CapEx that hyperscalers spend on AI chips and, at the same time, shift the majority of costs to its partners, such as Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which produce those chips. This makes it possible for Nvidia to generate significant returns without taking on too much risk.

Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) is another solid choice. After declaring 'code red' following the release of OpenAI's ChatGPT in late 2022, the company was able to release its own Gemini chatbot, which in recent months has started to gain traction and take market share from OpenAI. 

The integration of AI Overviews and AI Mode, which are powered by Gemini, into Google's search platform also made it possible for Alphabet to continue to dominate the search business in the era of LLMs. In addition, after developing state-of-the-art video and image generation models like Google Veo and Nano Banana, Alphabet could be considered an all-rounder AI company that has everything going for it to thrive in the age of AI.

When it comes to pure text-based LLM companies, Anthropic (ANTHRO) has my vote. Unlike OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic has been focused on providing its Claude chatbot mostly to enterprise clients. This made it possible for the company to experience a surge in revenue despite having fewer users than ChatGPT. 

Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 model quickly gained popularity, and there is speculation this prompted OpenAI to discontinue its video generation model Sora to focus on improving its text-based LLMs in light of increasing competition from Anthropic. Following the announcement of the Claude Mythos model, Anthropic might soon have the most capable LLM for general purposes in the world and pave the way for the AGI era.

Rick Orford: Right now, we've got a lot of uncertainty. With the war in Iran and rising oil prices, the Fed is signaling a willingness to raise rates once again, following a sector transition away from tech and into more defensive plays.

In this environment, I think the best option in AI is to play it safe. But, safe and AI doesn't have to mean huge upside—it can also mean hedging against the volatility found in the overly speculative names. 

I think the best AI company right now is Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL). With a gigantic backlog in cloud, growing ad revenue, and expanding margins, they've got the most viable business. And one only needs to look at what happened in the markets on April 8 to see how powerful the forces could be once a little good news comes our way.

data.title: Lufthansa pilots to strike over wage dispute
data.description:
Lufthansa AG (DLAKF) is bracing for another trade union action this month as a union representing its pilots called for a two-day strike on Saturday, citing the German carrier’s failure to resolve a wage dispute.

In a statement, labor union Vereinigung Cockpit announced that the walkout will begin on April 13 at 00:01 local time and conclude on April 14 at 23:59 local time, affecting flights operated by Lufthansa (DLAKY), Lufthansa Cargo, and its regional unit Lufthansa CityLine.

Flights from Germany to several Middle Eastern destinations will be exempt from the trade union action due to the ongoing military tensions in the region.

As for Lufthansa’s low-cost carrier Eurowings, flights departing from all German airports on April 13 will be impacted by the strike.

As reasons for the walkout, the union cited Lufthansa’s (DLAKF) failure to offer a pension scheme and Lufthansa CityLine’s refusal to sign a new collective bargaining agreement. Additionally, Eurowings’ proposed pension scheme was unacceptable, Vereinigung Cockpit said, adding that it was open to negotiations.

Lufthansa (DLAKF) saw hundreds of flight cancellations on Friday as its cabin crew walked out of their jobs, marking the third trade union action faced by Germany’s largest airline this year, following two pilot strikes in February and March.

data.title: Insider trades: Nike, Marvell Technology among notable names
data.description:
A weekly overview of insider trading, highlighting significant purchases and sales by investors, directors, and executives. The transactions occurred between April 6, 2026, and April 10, 2026.

  • Marvell Technology (MRVL) EVP and Chief Legal Officer Mark Casper sold 6,900 shares at $109.45 per share for $755,205, reducing his holdings by 32.57% to 14,286 shares.
  • Lululemon (LULU) Chief Brand Officer Nicole Neuburger sold 622 shares at $161 per share for $100,142, lowering her holdings by 3.14% to 19,156 shares.
  • Credo Technology (CRDO) CEO William Brennan reported a sale worth $1.2M. Brennan let go of 12,298 shares at $101.45 each, diluting his holding by 0.61% to 2M.
  • Nebius’ (NBIS) Chief Infrastructure Officer Andrey Korolenko sold 31,357 shares at $111.20 per share, generating $3.49M and cutting his stake by 5.09% to 585,209 shares.
  • Ulta Beauty (ULTA) director George Mrkonic reduced his holdings by nearly 16% to 2,383 shares. Mrkonic sold 452 for $539.29 each, for a total worth of $243,759.
  • Nike (NKE) director Robert Swan purchased 11,781 shares at $42.44 per share, totaling $500,002 and increasing his holdings to 56,654 shares.
  • Victoria’s Secret & Co. (VSCO) director Anne Sheehan acquired 4,842 shares at $45.86 per share for $222,054, bringing her total stake to 34,261 shares. Meanwhile, director Donna James bought 2,175 shares for $46.23 each, for a total value of $100,550, increasing her holdings to 63,097 shares.

data.title: Direct negotiations between U.S. and Iran start in Pakistan
data.description:
Direct negotiations between delegations from the U.S. and Iran began in the Pakistani capital of Islamabad on Saturday under a two-week ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran.

Citing sources in Islamabad, Al Jazeera reported that the two teams are negotiating directly in the presence of Pakistani mediators. Previously, the U.S. and Iranian teams were expected to communicate with each other from separate rooms, with Pakistani officials anticipated to pass messages between them.

Earlier in the day, Pakistan confirmed the arrival of the Iranian delegation led by the speaker of parliament, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in Islamabad.

A few hours later, U.S. Vice President JD Vance landed in Islamabad. He was met by U.S. Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and President Donald Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner.

The meeting comes six weeks after the U.S. and Israeli military campaign in Iran began in late February, sending oil prices sharply higher amid Tehran’s near-total blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about 20% of global seaborne oil trade normally takes place.

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA Asks: Is now a good time to buy solar stocks?
data.description:
Given the ongoing volatility in the global oil and gas markets, is now a good time to buy solar energy stocks?

Seeking Alpha analysts WideAlpha and Ragmar Rikberg weigh in. 

WideAlpha: The boring answer is “it depends.” In general, we do not like PV manufacturers, as it is a very commoditized industry. We would therefore avoid the Invesco Solar ETF (TAN), as it has a high concentration in this segment of the solar sector.

We prefer indirect exposure through advanced system components, such as Enphase Energy (ENPH) with its microinverters and batteries. 

Nextpower (NXT) is also interesting, even if utility solar trackers are somewhat commoditized. They have found ways to differentiate themselves with things like their TrueCapture software that optimizes positioning and increases yield. Nextpower (NXT) is also expanding into other areas, from foundations to automatic monitoring/cleaning, and soon to power inverters.

Another option is companies that own and develop solar and wind projects. Some that we have written about in the past have been taken private, such as Innergex and Atlantica Yield. More recently, Boralex (BRLXF) also announced it is being taken private. Pure plays remaining in the sector include Brookfield Renewable (BEP) (BEPC) and Northland Power (NPIFF). 

The solar/wind producer we find most attractive, ERG SpA (ERGSY), trades on the Milan exchange. The company has wind and solar assets in several European countries, including Italy, Germany, and the U.K. It also has a dividend yield of ~4.5%, and its valuation multiples are below its historical averages. ERG has several opportunities in the development pipeline, including wind repowering, utility solar, and battery storage projects.

Ragmar Rikberg: Since U.S. natural gas prices haven’t risen alongside global gas prices—and given that natural gas remains the main source of electricity generation in the U.S. (about 40%, followed by nuclear at 18%)—I don’t see any particular catalyst for solar stocks in the U.S. right now.

That said, after analyzing First Solar (FSLR), I still found it to be an attractive investment, regardless of the current volatility in energy prices.

What surprised me during the FSLR analysis was that, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, renewables are projected to be the only electricity‑generation segment to grow in 2026, and that forecast was made months before the Iran crisis began.

In addition, I recently analyzed EV maker Rivian (RIVN)—not a pure solar play, but still connected to the broader renewable‑energy ecosystem. It could attract more attention as fuel prices at gas stations have spiked, and with the new R2 model coming out this spring.

  • Top Semiconductor Materials & Equipment Stocks
  • Top Electrical Components & Equipment Stocks

data.title: Trending stocks this week as ceasefire hopes lift sentiment amid U.S.-Israel-Iran Tensions
data.description:
Wall Street’s main indexes posted weekly gains this week as investors digested hopes of a ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Israel and Iran’s regime. For the week, the S&P gained 3.6%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite rose 4.7%, and the blue-chip Dow added 3.0%.

Applied Materials (AMAT) shares surged 8% Wednesday after the semiconductor equipment manufacturer revealed a pair of new chipmaking tools designed to operate with atomic-level precision for the new wave of AI chips. These new systems are designed to create the smallest features in the most advanced logic chips. The astronomical demand for artificial intelligence computing power has led to the introduction of gate-all-around transistors at 2nm.

Fastly (FSLY), along with its competitors Akamai (AKAM) and Cloudflare (NET), tumbled Friday after Anthropic (ANTHRO) launched its Claude Managed Agents. Fastly had plunged 22%, Akamai sunk 17%, and Cloudflare collapsed by 14%. Investors are apparently viewing Claude Managed Agents as a direct infrastructure competitor to these platforms.

Organon (OGN) closed 28% higher Friday after media reports from India indicated that Mumbai-based generic drugmaker Sun Pharmaceutical Industries had submitted a ~$12B binding bid for the company following months of due diligence, with financing support from JPMorgan Chase & Co. and MUFG.

Ares Management (ARES) declined 4% after reports that it is sounding out investors for a new ~$20B direct lending fund, even as rising redemption pressure weighs on sentiment across private credit. The firm recently capped withdrawals from its Ares Strategic Income Fund after receiving $1.2B in Q1 redemption requests, part of a broader ~$20B+ pullback from the asset class impacting peers like Apollo Global Management, Ares, Blackstone, Blue Owl Capital, and KKR, signaling growing investor caution despite continued fundraising efforts.

Medicare insurers rallied after the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services said it will raise average Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48% for 2027, amounting to more than $13B in additional funding. The move lifted insurer stocks, including Elevance Health (ELV) +3%, UnitedHealth Group (UNH) +9%, CVS Health (CVS) +7%, and Centene Corporation (CNC) +3%, as investors priced in improved reimbursement outlooks.

data.title: Abbott ordered to pay $70M in damages in Chicago baby formula case
data.description:
A jury in Chicago on Friday increased the total damages that Abbott Laboratories (ABT) must pay in a case alleging that the company’s baby formula caused a fatal bowel disease in preterm infants up to $70M.

After a months-long trial, the jurors in Illinois state court on Thursday ordered the MedTech giant to pay $53M in compensatory damages to four mothers who accused the company of hiding that its formula for premature infants can cause necrotizing enterocolitis ((NEC)).

The jurors reconvened on Friday to impose an additional $17M in punitive damages on Abbott (ABT). The company said it disagreed with the verdict and would appeal.

"Science was ignored in this case," the maker of Similac formulas said in a statement, adding, “This verdict, and the continued pursuit of a theory at odds with regulators and the medical community, risks eliminating vital options for doctors and the most vulnerable infants.”

The verdict marked the second trial loss for the Abbott Park, Illinois-based firm, which faces more than 1,700 similar lawsuits in federal and state courts that could potentially cost the company billions of dollars in additional damages.
